title: AI's Hidden Costs: Environmental Attack Vectors

modules.title: Module 1: The Resource Footprint of AI — Why Energy, Water, and Hardware Are Now Security Concerns
modules.sub: Mapping AI's physical dependencies as emerging security vulnerabilities
modules.description: Learners survey the scale of energy, water, and hardware consumption behind modern AI systems and reframe those dependencies as exploitable attack surfaces. They examine real-world examples of infrastructure strain and evaluate why traditional security perimeters have failed to account for these physical inputs. By the end, learners can articulate why environmental resources belong in any serious AI risk conversation.

modules.title: Module 2: Environmental Threat Vectors — How Adversaries Exploit AI Infrastructure Dependencies
modules.sub: Identifying attack paths that run through power, cooling, and facilities
modules.description: Learners analyze how adversaries target the physical and environmental layers of AI infrastructure, including power grid dependencies, cooling system disruptions, and facility-level access exploitation. They work through attack scenario walkthroughs to identify which dependencies introduce the highest risk exposure. Learners leave with a structured vocabulary for describing and categorizing environmental threat vectors.

modules.title: Module 3: Supply Chain and Hardware Risks — GPU Shortages, Counterfeit Components, and Vendor Lock-In
modules.sub: Assessing hardware supply chain fragility and its security consequences
modules.description: Learners investigate how GPU scarcity, counterfeit hardware, and concentrated vendor relationships create compounding security risks in AI procurement pipelines. They evaluate case studies of supply chain compromise and consider how vendor lock-in limits an organization's ability to respond to hardware-based threats. Learners develop criteria for vetting hardware suppliers and flagging supply chain red flags.

modules.title: Module 4: Cryptojacking and Compute Hijacking — When Your AI Workloads Fund Someone Else's Operations
modules.sub: Detecting and responding to unauthorized consumption of AI compute resources
modules.description: Learners explore how cryptojacking and compute hijacking attacks operate within AI environments, from compromised cloud credentials to rogue containers draining GPU capacity. They practice identifying behavioral and telemetry indicators that distinguish legitimate AI workloads from unauthorized usage. By the end, learners can recommend detection controls and initial response steps tailored to AI compute environments.

modules.title: Module 5: Regulatory and Compliance Landscape — Environmental Risk Disclosure and AI Governance
modules.sub: Navigating disclosure requirements where environmental impact meets AI accountability
modules.description: Learners examine the evolving regulatory landscape around AI environmental impact, including emerging disclosure obligations, ESG reporting expectations, and sector-specific governance frameworks. They assess how compliance gaps in environmental risk reporting can create both legal exposure and reputational harm. Learners map relevant regulations to their organizational context and identify where current governance policies may fall short.

modules.title: Module 6: Hardening AI Infrastructure — Monitoring Compute, Energy Anomalies, and Supply Chains
modules.sub: Applying concrete controls to detect and reduce environmental attack exposure
modules.description: Learners select and configure monitoring approaches for detecting anomalies in compute consumption, energy usage, and hardware integrity across AI infrastructure. They evaluate tooling options and policy levers — from workload telemetry to vendor audits — and prioritize controls based on organizational risk profile. The module culminates in a hardening checklist learners can adapt and apply directly within their environments.

modules.title: Module 7: Building an Environmental Risk Framework for AI Systems in Your Organization
modules.sub: Designing a repeatable organizational framework for AI environmental risk governance
modules.description: Learners synthesize course concepts into a structured environmental risk framework tailored to their organization's AI footprint, stakeholder landscape, and governance maturity. They define roles, escalation paths, and review cadences that keep environmental risk visible alongside traditional cybersecurity concerns. Learners leave with a draft framework document and a prioritized action plan they can present to decision-makers.

synopsis: The physical and environmental resources that power AI systems — electricity, water cooling, specialized hardware — have become exploitable attack surfaces that most security teams aren't watching, creating vulnerabilities that range from cryptojacking to supply chain sabotage. This course exposes the overlooked intersection of AI infrastructure and environmental risk, giving both general audiences and security professionals concrete strategies to identify exposure, respond to abuse, and factor resource security into AI governance.
rationale: As AI workloads consume unprecedented compute and energy resources, adversaries are actively targeting these dependencies through cryptojacking, hardware supply chain attacks, and infrastructure disruption — threat vectors that sit outside most organizations' current security models.
learning_outcome: Learners will be able to identify signs of unauthorized compute consumption in AI environments, assess environmental dependencies as attack surfaces, and apply a practical risk framework to harden AI infrastructure against resource-based exploitation.
source_signals: AI threatens to raid the water reserves of Europe's driest regions
nearest_existing: Why AI Systems Are Attack Surfaces
